narrative: Dear [Student's Name], as a Black woman applying to law school, you've shown determination in pursuing this path despite challenges, and I want to help you understand the automated decision that led to your application's rejection in a clear way. The admissions model looks at patterns from thousands of past students to predict whether someone is likely to pass the bar exam on their first try, and unfortunately, it estimated a 56.8% chance that you would not—which crossed the threshold for denial, as the school prioritizes applicants predicted to succeed right away. Your profile includes a solid LSAT score of 36, an undergraduate GPA of 2.50, Year 1 and Year 3 school deciles both at 2, lower-middle family income, and plans to attend full-time. When comparing you to typical students, your LSAT is close to the average of 36.7, but your GPA sits below the usual 3.21, your deciles are much lower than the averages around 5.5-5.7, and your family income is on the lower side of what's common. The most influential factor pulling in a positive direction—toward predicting you'd pass—was your LSAT score, which stood out as a strength relative to others. However, your Year 3 decile had the strongest push toward predicting failure, as being in the lower ranks there signaled potential struggles ahead. Your undergraduate GPA and Year 1 decile followed closely, both contributing notably toward the failure prediction because they fell short of what the model sees in successful bar takers. Meanwhile, your lower-middle family income and full-time enrollment plans offered smaller boosts toward passing, but not enough to offset the others. This combination made the model lean toward caution, even though many students with similar backgrounds have overcome odds through extra preparation. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost it further, strengthening your GPA through post-bac courses or highlighting upward trends in your deciles, and gathering letters that emphasize your resilience as a Black woman in this field. You're not defined by this prediction—many reapply successfully after addressing key areas, so use this as motivation to build an even stronger case next time. Keep pushing; your story deserves to be told in the courtroom.
